name: Daniel W. Whittle
hymns: Have You Any Room for Jesus?
I Know Whom I Have Believed
source_summary: Daniel Webster Whittle (1840–1901) was an American evangelist and gospel hymn writer associated with D.L. Moody's revival campaigns. Born in Chicopee Falls, Massachusetts, he was wounded and converted during the Civil War, subsequently working at the Elgin Watch Company before entering full-time evangelism in 1873. He partnered with Philip P. Bliss and later James McGranahan for revival campaigns across the United States and Great Britain, writing the texts for hymns set to music by his collaborators.
adult_story:
Daniel Webster Whittle was born in Chicopee Falls, Massachusetts in 1840. He served in the Union Army during the Civil War, was wounded and taken prisoner, and during his recovery he read a New Testament his mother had placed in his kit. He came to faith in Christ in those weeks — a soldier's conversion, in pain, in a prison, with no certainty about what came next.

He returned to civilian life, working at the Elgin Watch Company. In 1873, under the influence of D.L. Moody, he entered full-time evangelistic work. He partnered with Philip P. Bliss — who set his texts to music — and later with James McGranahan, conducting revival campaigns across the United States and Great Britain for the rest of his life.

His hymn "I Know Whom I Have Believed" is built on 2 Timothy 1:12. It does not claim to understand everything — "I know not why God's wondrous grace / To me He hath made known" — but it asserts a knowledge that counts for everything: I know Christ. I have committed myself to Him. He will keep what I have entrusted to Him until the last day.

The hymn was written in a century that celebrated certainty and doubted it in equal measure. Whittle's answer was not intellectual certainty but personal trust: not a solved argument but a known Person, and a persuasion that He is able.

"Moment by Moment" — written with May Whittle Moody — sets out a theology of union with Christ made into something a congregation can carry through an ordinary day: "Dying with Jesus, His death reckoned mine / Living with Jesus, a new life divine / Looking to Jesus till glory doth shine / Moment by moment, O Lord, I am Thine."

Whittle died in 1901. A converted soldier who became an evangelist, he spent his ministry helping people understand that faith is not a feeling but a trust in a Person — one whose faithfulness makes our stumbling commitment secure.
family_story:
Daniel Whittle was born in America in 1840. When he was a young soldier in the Civil War, he was wounded and taken prisoner. While he was recovering, he read a New Testament his mother had given him — and he gave his life to Jesus.

After the war, Daniel worked at a watch company. Then in 1873 he began to travel across America and Britain, telling people about Jesus.

He wrote a hymn called "I Know Whom I Have Believed." It says that there is much we don't understand about God's ways — but there is one thing we can be sure of: we know Jesus, and He is able to keep us.

"I know not why God's wondrous grace
To me He hath made known,
Nor why, unworthy, Christ in love
Redeemed me for His own.
But I know Whom I have believed."

Daniel died in 1901. A soldier who became a preacher — showing that Jesus can meet us in the hardest and strangest places.

name: Elisha A. Hoffman
hymns: Are You Washed in the Blood?
source_summary: Elisha Albright Hoffman (1839–1929) was an American Presbyterian minister and prolific gospel hymn writer. Born in Orwigsburg, Pennsylvania, to an Evangelical Association minister, he was ordained a Presbyterian minister in 1873 after eleven years with the Evangelical Association's publishing house in Cleveland. He served his longest pastorate of thirty-three years at the First Presbyterian Church in Benton Harbor, Michigan. He composed over 2,000 hymns and edited more than fifty songbooks.
adult_story:
Elisha Albright Hoffman was born on 7 May 1839 in Orwigsburg, Pennsylvania. His father was an Evangelical Association minister, and the rhythm of ministry and Scripture shaped his childhood. He studied at Union Seminary, worked eleven years with the Evangelical Association's publishing house in Cleveland, and was ordained as a Presbyterian minister in 1873.

He pastored churches across the Midwest, his longest tenure — thirty-three years — at the First Presbyterian Church in Benton Harbor, Michigan. He composed over 2,000 hymns and edited more than fifty songbooks. He died in Chicago in 1929, having spent ninety years living through everything from the Civil War to the gramophone age.

His most well-known hymns survive because they do something direct and simple. "Are You Washed in the Blood?" is an invitation to examine one's standing before Christ: "Have you been to Jesus for the cleansing power? / Are you washed in the blood of the Lamb?" It is blunt in the way revival preaching was blunt — it assumes that no one present may have a settled understanding of their own position before God, and it calls them to the cross.

"Leaning on the Everlasting Arms" arose from a collaboration with Anthony J. Showalter. Showalter had written to Hoffman after receiving letters from two bereaved men in his congregation, quoting Deuteronomy 33:27 — "The eternal God is thy refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms." Together they produced a hymn whose chorus is among the most widely sung expressions of Christian confidence: "Leaning, leaning, safe and secure from all alarms / Leaning on the everlasting arms."

"I Must Tell Jesus" arose from a pastoral conversation. A woman in Hoffman's congregation told him of her troubles, asking what she should do. Hoffman replied simply: "You must tell Jesus." He went home and wrote the hymn. The most ordinary pastoral exchange became the premise of a song still sung by congregations who need permission to bring their actual circumstances to Christ.

Hoffman's hymns are not theological treatises. They are entry points — direct, clear invitations to come to Christ, to lean on Him, to bring Him what we carry.
family_story:
Elisha Hoffman was born in Pennsylvania in 1839, in a family where church and the Bible were the centre of everything. He grew up to become a minister, and then spent most of his life preaching and writing hymns — more than 2,000 of them.

One day a woman in his congregation was very sad and asked him what she should do. Elisha's answer was simple: "You must tell Jesus." Then he went home and wrote a hymn about it.

That is what many of his hymns do: they give us the simplest, most important instruction. Tell Jesus. Lean on Jesus. Ask whether your sins are washed in the blood of Jesus.

"Leaning on the Everlasting Arms" is one of his best loved:

"What a fellowship, what a joy divine,
Leaning on the everlasting arms."

Underneath us, like arms that never tire, is God himself holding us up.

Elisha spent his whole long life — he died at ninety — telling people to come to Jesus. Sometimes the most important thing is the simplest thing.

name: Frances Ridley Havergal
hymns: Take My Life, and Let It Be
Who Is on the Lord's Side?
source_summary: Frances Ridley Havergal (1836–1879) was an English Anglican hymn writer and poet. Born in Astley, Worcestershire, the daughter of clergyman and hymn writer William Henry Havergal, she was highly educated and had memorised the New Testament, the Psalms, and much of the Old Testament prophets. She experienced evangelical conversion around 1851 and a deeper consecration in 1873 that produced her most celebrated hymns. She died of peritonitis aged forty-two.
adult_story:
Frances Ridley Havergal was born in Astley, Worcestershire in 1836, the youngest child of William Henry Havergal — a clergyman, composer, and hymn writer. From childhood she was drawn to Scripture and language: she had memorised the New Testament, the Psalms, and much of the prophets before she was an adult, and she read Greek and Hebrew.

She came to evangelical faith around 1851 — what she described as a deliberate, decisive trust: "I did trust the Lord Jesus." In 1873, reading a booklet called *All For Jesus* deepened this into what she called full consecration — a total self-offering that became the wellspring of her most distinctive hymns.

"Take My Life, and Let It Be" is the outworking of that 1873 crisis. Verse by verse it moves through every faculty and possession — voice, hands, feet, intellect, will, heart, love, time, money: "Take my will, and make it Thine / It shall be no longer mine / Take my heart, it is Thine own / It shall be Thy royal throne." It does not leave the singer anywhere safe. Everything must be submitted. The hymn is a petition precisely because the submission is not natural to us.

"Like a River Glorious" gives the counterpoint — the peace that follows trust: "Stayed upon Jehovah, hearts are fully blest / Finding, as He promised, perfect peace and rest." The peace described is not an absence of difficulty but a settled trust in a God whose purposes are certain, whose love is unfailing.

"Who Is on the Lord's Side?" is a call to allegiance — but allegiance that rests not in human resolve but in what Christ has done: "Jesus, Thou hast bought us / Not with gold or gem / But with Thine own lifeblood / For Thy diadem."

Havergal died of peritonitis at forty-two in June 1879. Her final recorded words, on the last day of her life: "If I am going, it is too good to be true. Come, Lord Jesus, come quickly! Splendid to be so near the gates of heaven. So beautiful to go."

She had spent her life writing of consecration and trust. She died as she had written.
family_story:
Frances Ridley Havergal was born in England in 1836. Her father was a minister and hymn writer, and from a very young age she loved books, languages, and poetry. She had memorised much of the Bible before she was grown up.

When she was a teenager she gave her heart to Jesus — not just learning about Him, but truly trusting Him.

When she was in her thirties she felt God asking for her whole life — not just part of it. She said yes. Then she sat down and wrote one of the most loved hymns in the church:

"Take my life, and let it be
Consecrated, Lord, to Thee...
Take my will, and make it Thine,
It shall be no longer mine."

She wrote about trusting God through everything, even when life is hard. "Like a River Glorious" says that those who stay close to God find peace that goes deeper than feelings or circumstances.

Frances died very young — just forty-two years old. But as she was dying, she said: "So beautiful to go." She was not afraid. She was going to the One she had sung about all her life.
